title: PARS: reading the pairing state of RNA via the cleavage ratio of two nucleases

blocks.text: If you only look at the data table, PARS (Parallel Analysis of RNA Structure) easily reads the cleavage by its two nucleases as "a pairing call settled in one go" — as if the two columns of V1 and S1 readouts ultimately collapse into a black-and-white pairing conclusion. That understanding is wrong. PARS is an enzymatic probing method: rather than using one chemical reagent to modify a particular class of base, it uses two nucleases simultaneously to cleave the same stretch of RNA — RNase V1 cleaves double-stranded/paired regions (leaving a nick at the 3′ end of double-stranded RNA), and RNase S1 cleaves single-stranded/unpaired regions (leaving a nick at the 3′ end of single-stranded RNA). In other words, what PARS reports first is not "this base was modified", but "how many times this base was cleaved in the V1 experiment and how many times in the S1 experiment", and from the ratio of the two sets of cleavage (the V1/S1 ratio) it then infers whether the base is more like a duplex or a single strand.

This determines how FoldBridge should treat PARS data. The interpretable object of PARS is nuclease accessibility, not the ground truth of chemical modification; in principle all bases can be cleaved by the enzymes, and what actually carries the pairing information is the ratio of the two sets of V1 and S1 readouts. FoldBridge can store the raw V1 and S1 cleavage counts and can display the experimental signal on a web page; but the moment it enters the default structure colouring, the explanatory text or the confidence, a more specific question must be asked first: is this number, in PARS's enzymatic-cleavage context, a PARS score derived from the V1/S1 ratio that can be interpreted as paired/unpaired evidence? The raw cleavage count of a single nuclease must first pass the interpretive gate of the V1/S1 ratio before it can become a candidate for default colour or confidence.
The experimental chain: V1 cleaves duplexes, S1 cleaves single strands, and the ratio gives a per-base score

blocks.legend: **Figure 1. The core experimental principle of PARS.** The schematic of Kertesz et al. 2010 (PMID 20811459, DOI `10.1038/nature09322`) breaks PARS into two complementary experiments that are then combined: (a) the RNA molecule is first cleaved by RNase V1, which makes a nick at the 3′ end of double-stranded RNA, leaving a 5′ phosphate (5′P), with a red arrow in the figure marking one such cleavage; after random fragmentation, the fragments generated by V1 are specifically captured and deeply sequenced, and each aligned sequence provides one piece of structural evidence for a single base (the red box in the figure is the evidence from a single mapped read, and the grey box is the evidence from many more reads stacked together). A large number of reads aligning to the same base indicates that it was repeatedly cleaved by V1, and is therefore more likely to be in a double-stranded conformation. (b) The same as (a), but the RNA sample is treated with RNase S1 instead, which cleaves at the 3′ end of single-stranded RNA; in this case, the reads collected suggest that the base was unpaired in the original structure. (c) Combining the data from the two complementary experiments in (a) and (b) yields a single-nucleotide-resolution score representing the likelihood that the queried base is in a double-stranded or a single-stranded conformation.
body: PARS measures nuclease accessibility, and the pairing information is hidden in the ratio of the two sets of cleavage, not in the absolute cleavage amount of any one enzyme. Strong cleavage by V1 suggests a duplex, and strong cleavage by S1 suggests a single strand, but either set alone is not enough to conclude — only by putting V1 and S1 together to compute the ratio does one obtain the PARS score.

For FoldBridge, this means that the numbers in the final table are not a "structural label" handed over directly by one enzyme, but a measured quantity that, after V1 cleavage, S1 cleavage, capture, library preparation, sequencing and normalisation, has further passed through a ratio calculation. Taking the raw count of a single set of V1 or S1 as the ground truth of pairing skips the very V1/S1-ratio gate on which PARS actually relies for interpretation.
blocks.label: PARS F1
blocks.legendText: (a) RNA molecules are cleaved by RNase V1, which cuts 3′ of double-stranded RNA, leaving a 5′ phosphate (5′P). One such cut is illustrated by a red arrow. Following random fragmentation, V1-generated fragments are specifically captured and subjected to deep sequencing. Each aligned sequence provides structural evidence about a single base. The marked red square illustrates the evidence obtained from one mapped sequence (red). Additional evidence (gray boxes) is collected by mapping more sequences (gray horizontal bars). A large number of reads aligned to the same base indicates that the base is cleaved multiple times by RNase V1 and is thus more likely to be in double stranded conformation. (b) Same as (a), but when the RNA sample is treated with RNase S1, which cuts 3′ of single-stranded RNA. Collected reads in this case suggest that the base was unpaired in the original RNA structure. (c) By combining the data extracted from the two complementary experiments (a) and (b), we obtain a nucleotide-resolution score representing the likelihood that the inspected base was in a double- or single-stranded conformation.

blocks.text: Comparing against traditional footprinting: PARS signal must line up with the gold standard

blocks.legend: **Figure 2. Comparison of PARS signal with traditional footprinting.** Kertesz et al. 2010 (PMID 20811459, DOI `10.1038/nature09322`) validate PARS using specific transcripts: (a) the PARS signal of bases 50–110 of the yeast gene CCW12 — the double-strand-cleaving enzyme RNase V1 (red bars) and the single-strand-cleaving enzyme RNase S1 (green bars) — agrees closely with the traditional footprinting result for the same stretch of transcript (black line); the PARS signal is expressed as the number of reads aligning to each nucleotide, and the footprinting result comes from the semi-automated quantification of the RNase lanes in (b), with red arrows marking V1 cleavage and green arrows marking S1 cleavage. (b) The gel analysis of CCW12, comprising RNase V1 (lanes 5, 6), S1 (lanes 3, 4), the RNase T1 ladder (lanes 2, 8), alkaline hydrolysis (lanes 1, 9) and the no-RNase control (lane 7). (c) The PARS signal of bases 50–120 of the yeast gene RPL41A likewise agrees with traditional footprinting, and (d) is the corresponding V1/S1 gel. (e-f) The raw read counts of V1 (red bars) and S1 (green bars), and the PARS score (blue bars) derived from them, on a structural domain of ASH1 (e) and URE2 (f), with the known structure coloured according to the PARS score.
body: This figure places PARS in the right frame of reference: its output must be able to reproduce traditional footprinting, the gold standard. It is worth noting that the figure plots the V1 red bars, the S1 green bars and the merged PARS score blue bars together — this is precisely the visualisation that "the two sets of enzymatic-cleavage readouts" and "the ratio score" are not the same thing. Where the red bars are high suggests double-stranded accessibility, where the green bars are high suggests single-stranded accessibility, and the blue PARS score is the quantity, ratioed from the two, that is used for structural interpretation.

For FoldBridge, this comparison line reminds us: the PARS score is trustworthy because it can line up with independent footprinting evidence; but this trustworthiness belongs to the score after the ratio, and not to any single V1 or S1 count on its own. Colouring a single-enzyme signal that has not been ratioed amounts to discarding the very step that lets PARS stand up.
blocks.label: PARS F2
blocks.legendText: (a) The PARS signal obtained for bases 50-110 of the yeast gene CCW12 using the double-stranded cutter RNase V1 (red bars) or single-stranded cutter RNase S1 (green bars) accurately matches the signals obtained by traditional footprinting of that same transcript domain (black lines). PARS signal is shown as the number of sequence reads which mapped to each nucleotide; footprinting results are obtained by semi-automated quantification of the RNase lanes shown in (b). The red arrows indicate RNase V1 cleavages and the green arrows indicate RNase S1 cleavages as shown in the gel (b). (b) Gel analysis of RNase V1 (lanes 5,6) and S1 (lanes 3,4) probing of CCW12. Additionally, RNase T1 ladder (lanes 2,8), alkaline hydrolysis (lanes 1,9), and no RNase treatment (lane 7) are shown. (c) The PARS signal obtained from bases 50-120 of the yeast gene RPL41A matches the signals obtained by traditional footprinting. (d) RNase V1 (lanes 5,6) and S1 (lanes 7,8) probing of RPL41A, RNase T1 ladder (lane 2), alkaline hydrolysis (lanes 1,9), and no RNase treatment (lane 4). (e-f) Raw number of reads obtained using RNase V1 (red bars) or RNase S1 (green bars) and the resulting PARS score (blue bars) along one inspected domain of ASH1 (e) and URE2 ( f ). Also shown are the known structures of the inspected domains with nucleotides color-coded according to their computed PARS score.

blocks.text: The ratio maps onto structural and sequence features: from predicted probability to codon periodicity

blocks.legend: **Figure 3. The correspondence of the PARS score with computational prediction and with transcript structural features.** Kertesz et al. 2010 (PMID 20811459, DOI `10.1038/nature09322`) test PARS at the transcriptome-wide scale: (a) there is a significant correspondence between PARS and computational predictions of RNA structure — the authors take the 3000 yeast mRNAs used in the Vienna package folding analysis, extract the predicted double-stranded probability of each nucleotide, sort the nucleotides by PARS score (x-axis) and plot the mean predicted double-stranded probability (y-axis); the grey is the mean and standard deviation of 1000 shuffling experiments (assigning a random score to each probed base). (b) The discrete Fourier transform of the mean PARS score across the coding region, 3′ UTR and 5′ UTR, with the inset showing the mean PARS score at each of the three codon positions. (c) The mean PARS score across the 5′ UTR, coding region and 3′ UTR, with the transcripts aligned to the translation start and stop sites respectively (left and right columns), grey bars marking the start/stop codons, and horizontal lines indicating the mean PARS score of each region.
body: This figure puts the value of the PARS score in its proper place: once sorted by ratio, the mean predicted double-stranded probability changes monotonically, and it can also reproduce the three-base periodicity at the codon level within the coding region. These regularities show that the PARS score carries genuine structural and sequence information, not noise.

But this is also precisely where it is easiest to overstep. All the conclusions here are built on the PARS score (the V1/S1 ratio), and not on any single set of enzymatic-cleavage counts. A high V1 count at a base may mean a duplex, or it may merely mean the locale is more easily accessed by the enzyme; only by putting it together with the S1 count at the same site can one speak of a pairing inference. For FoldBridge, translating a single-enzyme raw count directly into "paired here" or "unpaired here" skips the very ratio quantity that PARS actually measures and sorts by.
blocks.label: PARS F3
blocks.legendText: (a) Significant correspondence between PARS and computational predictions of RNA structure. We used the Vienna package 12 to fold the 3000 yeast mRNAs used in our analysis, and extracted the predicted double-stranded probability of each nucleotide. Shown is the average predicted double-stranded probability of each nucleotide (y-axis), where nucleotides were sorted by their PARS score (x-axis). Average and standard deviation from 1000 shuffle experiments in which a random prediction score was assigned to each probed base are shown in gray. (b) Discrete Fourier transform of average PARS score across the coding region, 3′ UTR and 5′ UTR. Inset shows PARS score obtained for each of the three positions of every codon, averaged across all codons. (c) PARS score across the 5′ UTR, the coding region, and the 3′ UTR, averaged across all transcripts used in our analysis. Transcripts were aligned by their translational start and stop sites for the left and right panel, respectively; start and stop codons are indicated by gray bars; horizontal bars denote the average PARS score per region.

blocks.text: The ratio can connect to function: the relationship of structure to translation and secretion signals

blocks.legend: **Figure 4. The link between RNA structure and translational function revealed by PARS.** Kertesz et al. 2010 (PMID 20811459, DOI `10.1038/nature09322`) link the PARS score to functional data: (a) a sliding-window analysis of the relationship between the local PARS score and ribosome density, showing the significance (p value) of the inverse correlation between the mean PARS score within a 40 bp window and the reported ribosome density. (b) Left: for transcripts with sufficient coverage, k-means clustering of the PARS score within a ±40 bp window around the translation start site, with the right giving the mean structural profile of each cluster and the number of member genes; right: the cumulative distribution of ribosome occupancy for each cluster, and the p value of the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test between the distributions of cluster 1 and cluster 4. (c) ORFs of predicted secreted proteins tend to have less structure in the first ~30 bases of the coding region — structure usually accumulates immediately upon entering the coding sequence (CDS), but genes predicted to encode secreted proteins keep low structure in the first ~30 bases of the CDS, consistent with the dual-function SSCR being structurally more like a UTR than a CDS; the figure plots the 30 bp sliding-window mean relative PARS score for 499 secreted-protein genes (blue), the remaining 2501 genes (green) and the mean and standard deviation of 1000 random draws of 499 genes (grey).
body: This figure shows that PARS's usefulness goes beyond reproducing a stretch of structure: it can also link the structural signal to functional phenomena such as translational efficiency and secretion signal sequences. The precondition for doing these analyses is, again, that what is in hand is the PARS score — a quantity derived from the V1/S1 ratio that is comparable across transcripts.

For FoldBridge, the conclusion is direct: these elegant functional associations all hold at the level of the ratio score. If a single-enzyme signal that has not been ratioed is stuffed into the same analysis or the same confidence channel, it can neither align with footprinting nor reproduce these functional regularities — once the ratio gate is bypassed, the part that makes PARS PARS disappears.
blocks.label: PARS F4
blocks.legendText: (a) Sliding window analysis of local PARS score and ribosome density 16 . Shown is the significance (p-value) of the anti-correlation between average PARS score along a 40bp-wide window and the reported ribosome density. (b) Left: k -means clustering of PARS scores across the ±40bp window surrounding the translation start site of all transcripts for which enough coverage was obtained. The average structural profile and number of member genes is shown to the right of each cluster. Right: Cumulative distribution plot of ribosome occupancy for each cluster and the associated Kolmogorov-Smirnoff test p-value between the distribution of cluster 1 and 4. (c) Tendency for less RNA structure in the first 30 bases of ORFs encoding predicted secretory proteins. While structure typically builds up immediately upon entry to the coding sequence (CDS), genes predicted to code for secretory proteins retain low structure in the first ~30 bases of the CDS, consistent with the dual function SSCR having structural features of UTR rather than CDS 20 . Shown are the average relative PARS scores (Methods) across a 30bp sliding window for the 499 genes coding for secretory proteins (blue), the remaining 2501 genes (green) and the mean and standard deviation obtained from 1000 shuffle experiments in which sets of 499 genes were randomly selected (gray).

blocks.text: How FoldBridge should write about PARS

PARS's core rule in FoldBridge is to treat the V1/S1 ratio as the source of paired/unpaired evidence, rather than treating the raw cleavage count of either set of nucleases as structural ground truth. The V1 count and the S1 count are first of all provenance: they record how many times, under a given cleavage condition, a given base was cleaved by the double-strand-cleaving enzyme and the single-strand-cleaving enzyme respectively. Only when these two sets of readouts are paired into a ratio at the same site, yielding the PARS score, does this signal enter the default visualisation and confidence.

The key to this boundary is to separate "was cleaved by the enzyme" from "pairing state". PARS is an enzymatic method, all bases can in principle be approached by the enzymes, and heavy cleavage by one enzyme alone cannot conclude: strong V1 cleavage suggests a duplex, but local conformation, enzyme accessibility and sequence preference all affect the absolute cleavage amount; strong S1 cleavage suggests a single strand, equally perturbed by these factors. What actually cancels out most of the shared bias and leaves the pairing information is the ratio of V1 to S1. What PARS measures is "the relative accessibility of this base across two complementary enzymatic cleavages", and from this it infers whether the base is more like a duplex or a single strand.

FoldBridge therefore needs a gate concept, rather than a piece of rule code written out of thin air. The duty of this gate is to confirm, before sending the enzymatic-cleavage signal into the default structure colouring or confidence, that it comes from a properly paired V1/S1 ratio (rather than an isolated single-enzyme count), that the V1 and S1 experiments both have sufficient coverage at the site, that it has been reliably mapped to structural coordinates, and that it is interpreted as paired/unpaired evidence based on the nuclease-accessibility ratio. Only a PARS score that passes the gate enters the default visual track and the confidence candidates; single-enzyme counts that do not pass are retained as raw provenance, debugging information or download-table fields.

This is not a weakening of PARS. On the contrary, it is a respect for its strength. PARS uses two complementary nucleases and relies on the ratio to distinguish paired from unpaired, both lining up with traditional footprinting and reproducing structural and functional regularities at the transcriptome-wide scale; the error occurs when "this enzyme cleaves a lot here" is extended directly into "this is definitely paired/unpaired here". So for the non-specialist reader, the most fitting statement is "PARS uses the cleavage ratio of two nucleases to turn whether a base is more like a duplex or a single strand into a sequencing-readable clue". A more complete sentence should be written as: in the PARS assay, the V1/S1 ratio at this site gives a PARS score leaning towards a duplex (or a single strand); if the coverage, mapping and context of both enzymes pass the checks, this score can serve as support for structural interpretation. This sentence is a little wordier than "a high V1 signal equals pairing", but it is scientifically more robust and more suited to entering FoldBridge's long-term knowledge base.
There is no real FoldBridge accession here, so the following is a clearly labelled illustrative scenario, used to show how a single PARS profile splits into three different layers.

Imagine a PARS profile flowing through FoldBridge's three layers. The first layer is the raw observation: the read counts at which each base on a stretch of yeast mRNA was cleaved in the V1 experiment and the S1 experiment, preserved together with the provenance of the cleavage conditions, capture, library preparation and sequencing. This layer only answers "what was recorded in the raw data" — it has two columns of cleavage counts, not a pairing label with the conclusion already drawn.

The second layer is the visual signal: only when the V1 and S1 counts at the same site are paired into a ratio, yielding the PARS score, and that score has been reliably mapped to structural coordinates and interpreted as a paired/unpaired clue based on nuclease accessibility, does it enter the default structure colouring. The colour a reader sees on the page should answer "within PARS's interpretive boundary, is this base more like a duplex or a single strand", not "how many times did one enzyme cleave here". Sites with only one-sided coverage (for example only V1, lacking S1), unable to form a reliable ratio, should not be mixed into this layer pretending to be the same kind of evidence.

The third layer is the confidence candidate: only a PARS score that has passed ratioing, two-sided coverage, mapping and context checks can possibly enter confidence. Even when they come from the same profile, the raw V1/S1 counts, the visualisation colour and the confidence travel three different paths.

What this illustrative scenario aims to show is system behaviour, not a particular set of numbers: a single PARS profile simultaneously grows the raw pair of enzymatic-cleavage counts, the visualised ratio signal and the confidence candidate, and these three layers each have their own semantics and cannot stand in for one another. If an article kneads them together, the reader will take a single column of enzymatic-cleavage counts as the final answer — especially reading "V1 cleaves a lot here" directly as "this base has already been judged paired".

So the correct ending for a PARS article is not "heavy V1 cleavage proves the base is paired", but rather: PARS data can provide powerful paired/unpaired clues for RNA structure interpretation; in FoldBridge, such clues become part of the structural interpretation and confidence only after first being interpreted by the V1/S1 ratio as a pairing tendency (rather than coming from a single-enzyme raw count) and passing the subsequent gates.
